coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide modified self-assembled S@C3N4/B@C3N4 metal-free nano-photocatalyst for high performance degradation of chloramphenicol
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Amit
Kumari, Anu
Sharma, Gaurav
Du, Bing
Naushad, Mu.
Stadler, Florian J.
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride based photocatalytic materials have proved to be one of most promising candidates for hazardous pollutant removal and clean energy production. Structural modification and engineering of various materials based on g-C3N4 have been attempted to achieve solar energy harvesting, slow charge transfer and efficiency. Here we report synthesis of all metal free self-assembled carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide layers modified S@g-C3N4/B@g-C3N4 (CRSB) photocatalyst for visible and solar degradation of chloramphenicol (CMP) (10 mg L−1). Differently doped g-C3N4 with different band structure forms a junction which grows into an effective Z-scheme photocatalyst. Extended visible absorption is achieved with optimized modification by reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and carbon quantum dots. The optical response, band structure and charge separation efficiency were tested by optical absorption, electrochemical impedance, Mott-Schottky plots and photo-luminescence. CQDs convert a typical type-II mechanism into effective Z-scheme transfer. For the best sample CRSB 99.1% CMP was photo-degraded in 90 min exposure to visible light and retention of high activity in natural solar light (92.4% in 120 min). A stable heterojunction has been attempted to design using boron doped g-C3N4 and sulphur doped g-C3N4 with different band positions via ratio optimization with enhanced charge transfer. The degradation rate for CRSB (0.0810 min−1) is 10 times than bare g-C3N4 (0.00802 min−1). The optimized combination of carbon quantum dots and RGO with intimate interfacial contact for charge flow & reduced recombination, better adsorption of pollutant, high visible absorption and production of reactive oxygen species. The effect of pH, electrolytes and water matrix was studied for CMP degradation. The mechanism of degradation and mineralization was confirmed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis, scavenging experiments and electron spin resonance. It was observed that the chances of •O2 – and •OH radicals increased on the formation of the junction as compared to individual doped and bare carbon nitride. The mineralization was ensured by high total organic removal and cyto-toxicity studies of treated water sample onto human peripheral blood (PBL) cells with high cell viability equivalent to untreated cells. This experiment supports the competence of optimally designed metal free and environmentally friendly cost-effective catalysts with higher photocatalytic efficiency for removal of hazardous pollutants.

Reusability, TOC analysis and cyto-toxicity analysis

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide modified self-assembled S@C3N4/B@C3N4 metal-free nano-photocatalyst for high performance degradation of chloramphenicol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride based photocatalytic materials have proved to be one of most promising candidates for hazardous pollutant removal and clean energy production. Structural modification and engineering of various materials based on g-C3N4 have been attempted to achieve solar energy harvesting, slow charge transfer and efficiency. Here we report synthesis of all metal free self-assembled carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxide layers modified S@g-C3N4/B@g-C3N4 (CRSB) photocatalyst for visible and solar degradation of chloramphenicol (CMP) (10 mg L−1). Differently doped g-C3N4 with different band structure forms a junction which grows into an effective Z-scheme photocatalyst. Extended visible absorption is achieved with optimized modification by reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and carbon quantum dots. The optical response, band structure and charge separation efficiency were tested by optical absorption, electrochemical impedance, Mott-Schottky plots and photo-luminescence. CQDs convert a typical type-II mechanism into effective Z-scheme transfer. For the best sample CRSB 99.1% CMP was photo-degraded in 90 min exposure to visible light and retention of high activity in natural solar light (92.4% in 120 min). A stable heterojunction has been attempted to design using boron doped g-C3N4 and sulphur doped g-C3N4 with different band positions via ratio optimization with enhanced charge transfer. The degradation rate for CRSB (0.0810 min−1) is 10 times than bare g-C3N4 (0.00802 min−1). The optimized combination of carbon quantum dots and RGO with intimate interfacial contact for charge flow & reduced recombination, better adsorption of pollutant, high visible absorption and production of reactive oxygen species. The effect of pH, electrolytes and water matrix was studied for CMP degradation. The mechanism of degradation and mineralization was confirmed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis, scavenging experiments and electron spin resonance. It was observed that the chances of •O2 – and •OH radicals increased on the formation of the junction as compared to individual doped and bare carbon nitride. The mineralization was ensured by high total organic removal and cyto-toxicity studies of treated water sample onto human peripheral blood (PBL) cells with high cell viability equivalent to untreated cells. This experiment supports the competence of optimally designed metal free and environmentally friendly cost-effective catalysts with higher photocatalytic efficiency for removal of hazardous pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: The universal issues of resource depletion and environmental contamination have deepened because of unplanned anthropogenic activities, industrial revolution and poor policies for control and monitoring in developing economies. Due to increasing water pollution and spreading of dreadful diseases, contamination control and environmental clean-up has been one of the key objectives of Governments, environmentalists and researchers. During recent years, pharmaceuticals especially antibiotics and their derivatives have been identified as emerging pollutants which origin from human and animal medicinal treatments [1,2]. As per estimates, 90% of consumed antibiotics are excreted [3] which are afterwards released into soil and water bodies. Antibiotics play an important role in human health protection and veterinary medicinal treatment [4] for bacterial disinfection and growth promotion [5]. The occurrence of antibiotics in water bodies and drinking supplies has thus caused serious concern because benefits of antibiotics are considered more vital than their toxicity. Their presence enhances the bacterial resistance and as per reports this is spreading at an alarming rate [6,7]. It has been estimated that about 0.7 million deaths occur per year because of increasing antibiotic resistance and this will surely have a quantum leap in coming years [8].

Chloramphenicol (CMP) is a popular antibiotic having nitro and chlorine substituents [9] and has been found to be resistant to conventional biological treatment methods. It has been often detected in ground, surface water and drinking supplies too because of high usage and improper disposal [10]. There are several reports that acute exposure to unwanted CMP may cause health hazards as anemia, marrow aplasia and visual impairment. The ever-increasing rise in antibiotic resistant bacteria due to antibiotics proliferation in water bodies has led to increase in interest for new materials and technologies for their removal [11,12]. Conventional treatment methods have low efficiency for removal of antibiotics and various other pharmaceutical effluents [13,14]. This is because of the fact that most of organic pollutants are resistant to environmental and biological degradation [15].

Semiconductor photocatalysis has the potential of utilizing natural solar light for removal of various organic pollutants without use of any additional chemical reagents and external energy [16,17]. Visible and solar powered photocatalysis embraces promise for fabricating next-generation materials and systems for sustainable water treatment and energy production [18]. However, progress in developing solar active and highly visible response photocatalysts with bio-compatibility and sustainability is still a middle challenge. In the recent years metal-free photocatalytic materials have been explored for solar and visible light supported organic pollutant removal and water splitting [19]. Carbon based materials with graphite like structure, just as graphene, carbon nitride (CN) and boron carbo nitride (BCN). In addition to high functionality carbonaceous materials can act as electron-acceptor which facilitates charge flow via reduced recombination of electrons and holes [20]. Among various metal-free semiconducting materials graphitic carbon nitride has become a hotspot in the recent years and is researched tremendously as a visible-light active photocatalyst [21]. But pure g-C3N4 has some drawbacks as insufficient sunlight activity and fast recombination of photo-induced charge carriers which leads to poor photocatalytic activity. Various attempts have been performed for improving the photocatalytic activity of carbon nitride by structural modification. Doping or embedment via metals and non-metals has been extensively used as an effective route to modify the band gap and electronic structure to enhance visible absorption and reduce recombination too [22,23]. Hu et al. (2018) substituted C by electron rich P atom which is capable to bond covalently to N atoms. The remaining electron on P can delocalize into triazine ring which develops into an electron rich position, boosting the photocatalytic activity. By S and P co-doping they improved the photocatalytic performance and a donor-acceptor concept was forwarded for reduced recombination and charge flow [24]. In addition forming heterojunctions [25–27], coupling with graphene/graphene oxide [28], modified g-C3N4 [29] or other materials as polymers [30]. As far as organic counterparts are concerned graphitic materials as graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide are considered as one of the best parts of hybrid catalytic systems. Among various organic counterparts for junctions, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) has found a special position because of higher electron mobility, conductivity and stability [31]. RGO also provides adsorption sites for pollutants, acts as a catalyst support and electron mediator. It has been reported that C quantum dots have up-conversion characteristics and can stimulate the visible light activity by photo-induced electron transfer [32] and interaction of electrons and oxygen. In addition, the band gap of g-C3N4 can also be narrowed by decorating with carbon dots [33].

In this report, a junction using S@C3N4 and B@C3N4 with different band structure was prepared and modified by RGO and decorated with carbon dots for photocatalytic degradation of chloramphenicol under visible light and sunlight. The formation of an effective junction between two differently doped carbon nitrides with reduced recombination and charge transfer. The study is significant because a highly optically active hetero-structure can be formed out of carbon nitride with no role of metallic catalysts. In addition, RGO provides a support matrix, increase the surface area and facilitates electron flow and mediation. Carbon quantum dots broaden the absorption spectrum, convert a type-II junction into an effective Z-scheme with protection of high potential bands and enhance the degradation of CMP remarkably.

The S@g-C3N4 was produced by facile condensation of thiourea (1 g) and urea (10 g) via heat treatment at 550 °C in a covered crucible at rate of 8 °C min−1 in muffle furnace under air atmosphere. Pure g-C3N4 was prepared by same method without thiourea. The materials were washed repeatedly with water+ethanol, dried and ground into fine powder. B@g-C3N4 was produced by calcination condensation of H3BO3 and urea by the same procedure as explained above. The quantities of thiourea and boric acid were selected as to obtain 3% of S and B in final g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S@C3N4/B@C3N4 (SB) and C quantum dots (CQD) decorated RGO/S@C3N4 /B@C3N4 (CRSB) junction
ce:section.ce:para: The typical construction of B@C3N4/S@C3N4 junction via a self-assembly method as: a suitable amount of powder of B@g-C3N4 (B@CN) and S@g-C3N4 (S@CN) was dispersed in CH3OH under 3 h ultrasonication. After further vigorous stirring for 6 h in a fume hood, the obtained powder was overnight dried at 50 °C. In this way a series of binary junction SB with varying content of S@g-C3N4 was prepared as SB-10 (10%), SB-20 (20%), SB-30 (30%), SB-40 (40%) and SB-50 (50%) were obtained. Reduced graphene oxide was synthesized by methodology in our previous reported work [34]. CQDs were synthesized by glucose carbonization in alkaline medium with 90 min ultrasonication (SUS304 stainless steel-2500 mL; frequency 42,000 Hz). In a typical procedure glucose (9 g) and NaOH (0.8 g) were dissolved in 40 mL distilled water under continuous stirring. With 90 min sonication, pH was adjusted to 7 by addition of HCl. The solution was then dialyzed for 24 h and dried to form C quantum dots. The ternary junction CRSB was prepared by same method with addition of varying amount of RGO (3%, 5%, 8% and 11%) in optimized catalyst SB-40 to form RGO/S@C3N4/B@C3N4 assembly. For decorating with CQDs, the assembly was dipped in CQDs solution and autoclaved at 180 °C for 24 h and sample was named as CRSB. The whole scheme for synthesis of CRSB is represented in Fig. 1 . CRSB with 8% optimized RGO ratio has been selected as best catalyst and thereby named as CRSB in whole discussion.

The crystalline structure was analyzed by a Rigaku, Japan, Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The FTIR spectra were obtained by Nicolet 5700 FTIR instrument using KBr pellet method. The surface morphology and microstructure was determined using Zeiss Gemini (SEM 500) scanning electron microscope (SEM) instrument and JEM-1230 (Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM) respectively. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to calculate surface area with Micromeritics (ASAP 2020) instrument. K-Alpha+ instrument by Thermo Fisher Scientific UK, was employed for elemental composition analysis of photocatalysts by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al Kα X-rays (1485.0 eV) at 10 mA and 15 kV (4.5 × 10−9 Torr pressure). Optical response behavior was observed and analyzed by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS) was with BaSO4 as reference using Hitachi U3900 instrument. The extent of recombination of electron holes was determined by photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra with L F4500 instrument (Hitachi, Japan). After photodegradation experiments, total organic carbon (TOC) of the reaction medium was calculated by TOC analyzer instrument (Shimadzu TOC-VCPH). The degradation intermediates of CMP were detected and separated using LCMS-IT-TOF instrument (Shimadzu) equipped with an electrospray ionization (EIS) source. For detecting the reactive oxygen species, Bruker ER200-SRC instrument was used for recording electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-Lpyrroline N-oxide) trapped •O2 – and •OH radicals.

The electrochemical response of prepared photocatalysts was studied via typical three electrode electrochemical workstation system (CHI 660D). The electrode system utilizes three electrodes viz. working electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and counter electrode. In a typical procedure (for fabricating working electrode as CRSB/ITO), 5 mg of photocatalytic material and 10 μL aqueous Nafion were dispersed in C2H5OH via sonication for 30 min. The obtained suspension was then dip-coated on the indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass (1 cm × 1.5 cm) and kept in oven at 100 °C for 8 h to prepare the working electrode. The electrochemical experiments were done with 0.05 M Na2SO4 by EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) under the open-circuit potential (AC voltage amplitude of 5.0 mV and frequency 0.01 Hz-100 kHz). The EIS (Nyquist) plots obtained were fitted into an equivalent circuit- 80 Ω RCT (charge transfer resistance) in parallel with RSCC (space charge capacitor as constant phase element) and 20 Ω RS (electrolyte resistance) in series. The flat band (FB) potential of bare g-C3N4, B@g-C3N4 and S@g-C3N4 for calculation of valence bands was calculated using Mott-Schottky plots from capacitance data.

The photocatalytic removal of pharmaceutical drug CMP as target pollutant (Structure shown in Fig. S1) using synthesized single, binary and ternary photocatalytic materials was tested by photo-degradation of under visible light and natural solar light (The solar experiments were performed at Solan, 30.924°N, 77.092°E in the Western Himalayan region, India). For visible light source a 300 W Xe lamp (with 450 mW cm−2 intensity) was used with cut-off filters (380–780 nm). The photocatalytic experiments were performed in a glass made TOPTION photo-reactor with a water circulation system to keep constant temperature (30 ± 0.5 °C). The photodegradation tests were also performed in sunlight. The photocatalyst e.g. CRSB (0.3 g L−1) was sonicated in the CMP suspension (10 mg L−1) and then subject to irradiation (15 cm from sample cell) in photo-reactor. For each timely analysis, a 20 μL aliquot was taken out from reaction slurry by Teflon syringe filter (0.22 μ) with centrifugation followed by filtration. The residual concentration of CMP was measured by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) instrument (SHIMADZU Japan) equipped a UV-DAD detector (Shimadzu, SPD-M20A) (Shimadzu) and C-18 column (5 μm, 245 × 4.5 mm) as stationary phase. As a mobile phase water/methanol mixture was used. The 20 μL sample aliquot from reaction slurry was eluted isocratically with water + methanol (50:50) with flow rate of 1 mL min−1.

Mass spectrometry (MS) was performed in positive ion mode with electro-spray ionization (ESI). The nebulization pressure was 35 psi and spray voltage was 5.5 keV. The radical scavenging experiments were performed for scavenging •OH, •O2 −, and h+ using isopropanol (IPA) 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), and ethylenediamminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) respectively.

The cyto-toxicity of CMP degraded products (photo-catalytically treated water with CRSB under visible light for 8 h experiments) was studied. The experiments were performed on Hi-media Ltd. procured human peripheral blood lymphocytes (PBL). MTT (methylthiazolyldiphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay method was used for the analysis [35]. In a typical procedure, cells were cultivated in medium (10% RPMI-1640) and subjected to 12 h incubation at 37 °C. The same procedure was done for bare cells, with the degraded products and positive control (ConA). 10 mL MTT assay was then added into wells (96 well plates) to generate formazan and incubated for 4 h again (5% CO2 atmosphere). The purple crystals were then dissolved in DMSO. The optical density (OD) was measured at 595 nm with microplate reader. The %viability was calculated and assayed in triplicate.

Fig. 2(a) represents the XRD pattern for C3N4, B@g-C3N4, S@g-C3N4 and CRSB photocatalysts. For bare carbon nitride the low-angle diffraction peak at 12.9° corresponds to (100) in-plane structural repeating unit (JCPDS 87–1526) [36]. The intense peak at 27.3° originates from periodic layers stacking along (002) plane axe with an inter-planar spacing d = 0.322 nm [37]. In the doped samples no new peaks appeared because of low dopant concentration, however increase in crystallinity can be observed. But in all doped g-C3N4 samples, the peaks are shifted slightly by 0.1–0.2° showing the successful incorporation of dopants. In the ternary catalyst CRSB (containing 8% RGO), the characteristic peaks for the carbon nitrides can be observed in addition to some additional peaks. The peaks at 23.8° and 42.8°, represent (002) and (101) diffraction planes for RGO. Because of low content of RGO, the intensity of peaks is low. A peak near 10° because of graphene oxide which may arise due to incomplete reduction of graphene oxide or a slight oxidation of RGO during the preparation of the composite catalyst. In addition, no peaks were recorded for CQDs which is probably because of amorphous character of CQDs, low content and high dispersion [38]. Further doping, formation of binary and ternary junctions was confirmed via FTIR, XPS and EDX analysis.

The FTIR spectra for S@g-C3N4, B@g-C3N4 and CRSB are shown in Fig. 2(b). The absorption bands between 1200 and 1650 cm−1 are because of heterocyclic CN and CN stretching modes of graphitic carbon nitride. The graphitic structure of g-C3N4 was confirmed by absorption peak at 804 cm−1 for triazine unit stretching mode [39]. The weak and broad band at 3201 cm−1 (S@g-C3N4) and 3204 cm−1 (B@g-C3N4) can be ascribed to NH stretching vibrational mode and bending mode of heptazine [40]. The peak at 1040 cm−1 is for the stretching mode of CS bond, confirming that sulphur is doped into the g-C3N4 lattice [41]. However, the BN vibration mode at 1414 cm−1 may be overlapped by CN stretching mode peak. For CRSB, in addition to characteristic bands of carbon nitrides, additional peaks can be observed. RGO exhibits peaks (low intensity) at 1733, 1367, 1013 cm−1 owing to stretching vibrations of CO, CO, C-OH bonds respectively. The intense peak at 1453 cm−1 in RGO shows that sp2 structure is restored. A peak near 1200 cm−1 confirms the reduction of –COOH and –OH groups of GO. The OH and NH stretching is observed in the broad bands in range 3000–3700 cm−1 [37]. This confirms successful formation of CRSB photocatalyst. Further confirmation is done in XPS and EDX studies.

X-ray photoelectron spectra for CRSB are presented in Fig. 3 . The main elements of C, N, S, B and O are detected in the survey scan for CRSB (Fig. 3a). Fig. 3(b) shows the high resolution C1s spectrum for displaying peaks at 283.6 eV, which is assigned to CC bonding in aromatic rings for g-C3N4 or CQDs [42,43]. The peak at 286.6 eV corresponds to sp2-bonded carbon to N (C=N) (for g-C3N4) or epoxy/hydroxyl (CO) in GO/RGO. The third peak at 287.3 eV is for carbonyl (C=O) respectively (GO/RGO). The maximum reduction of GO into RGO is confirmed by low intensity CO peaks. The C 1s peak for g-C3N4 (=C-C-) shifts towards lower binding energy values according to already reported values [44]. However it can be noticed that C1s (284.7 eV) standard peak has not displaced after formation of the junction. Fig. 3(c) shows the deconvoluted N 1s spectrum showing three peaks at contributions at 397.6, 398.2 and 399.4 eV corresponding to sp2-bonded N (C=N-C) of triazine rings, tertiary N (N–(C)3) and N bonded to H (C-N-H) respectively [45]. The O1s high resolution spectrum as shown in Fig. 3(d) is deconvoluted into peaks at 528.8 and 530.7 eV corresponding to oxygen in RGO and the adsorbed water OH groups respectively. These adsorbed OH groups have an edge during photocatalysis as they facilitate production of ●OH and ●O2 – radicals. The peaks may have slight positive or negative B.E shifts from values reported in literature for bare or pure moieties. The shifts in binding energy are interrelated with the changes in surface charge density because of electron transfers between semiconductors forming the interface. The presence of sulphur is confirmed by S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) with peaks at 164.6 and 166.4 eV owing to SN and N-S bonds in S doped CN [46]. The B 1s spectrum as given in Fig. 3(f) shows a single peak at 191.7 eV referring BN bond in B@g-C3N4 [47]. The XPS, XRD and FTIR analysis confirm the successful doping and formation of ternary CRSB assembly.

The morphologies and microstructure of CN and CRSB samples were characterized by SEM and TEM and corresponding images are shown in Fig. 4 . The SEM image for g-C3N4 (Fig. 4a) shows lamellar-structure nano-sheets with a smooth surface. However in case of CRSB catalyst, the surface becomes more rough and porous and has a layered morphology as observed in Fig. 4(b). Fig. 4(c) shows the TEM image of bare g-C3N4 showing a smooth nano-sheet structure. The hierarchal structure can be observed in Fig. 4(c) & (d) for CRSB with interfaces clearly visible between two differently doped carbon nitrides and reduced graphene oxide. The high resolution image (Fig. 4e) shows a nano-dimension assembly in seen with a high interfacial contact with carbon quantum dots also visible. The tight and stable interfacial contact and layered surface morphology has a large influence on the electron-hole mobility and separation. The elemental mapping (Fig. 5f-k) for CRSB catalyst shows the presence and distribution of constituent elements C, N. O, S and B confirming the uniform composition and formation of composite assembly.

The BET specific surface area of pristine g-C3N4 as calculated from N2 adsorption isotherms (Fig. S2) is 44.2 m2 g−1. The N2 adsorption for CRSB shows a type-IV isotherm with a H4/H3 hybrid hysteresis loop [48] with a high surface area of 124.2 m2 g−1. Bare g-C3N4 has a low pore size of 12.3345 nm and pore volume of 0.12643 cm3 g−1. This porosity increases when B@CN and S@CN form an assembly with RGO with pore size of 23.2126 nm and 0.26678 cm3 g−1 pore volume. The CRSB has a high surface area which facilitates more reactive sites and better adsorption of pollutants for enhanced photocatalytic oxidation.

The optical absorption properties were recorded using UV-DR spectroscopy and corresponding plots are represented in Fig. 5(a). Pristine g-C3N4 shows characteristic visible absorption edge near 480 nm [49]. On B doping, a red shift is observed with high absorption in visible region. A high red shift is observed in case of S doped g-C3N4. B and S indeed enhance the optical absorption as they can act as impurity centers. They create impurity bands between CB and VB and thus effectively reduce the band gap too. In addition they can also act as recombination of electron-holes to make charge carriers free at the CB and VB of the other semiconductor. In addition the dopants also have an effect on the surface charge of CN as measured by the zeta potentials. On B and S addition the zeta potential becomes more negative which means that stability is also increased. On formation of the junction SB-40 in addition to a large absorption edge shift a high absorption in extended visible range is also achieved. The enhanced spectral response is due to effective junction formation between the two semiconductors with different band parameters. The Kubelka-Munk plots (Fig. 5b) were used to calculate band gaps. The band gap of undoped g-C3N4 is calculated as 2.70 eV which falls slightly to 2.61 eV and 2.52 eV for B@CN and S@CN respectively. The band gap is further lowered to 2.52 eV for S@CN/B@CN (SB-40) and to 2.05 eV by introduction of RGO and CQDs in CRSB catalyst. Both the counterparts of the junction are formed of carbon nitride. Doping via B and S however changes the scenario. The band positions are shifted to the extent that the two differently doped carbon nitrides behave as different semiconductors to form an effective heterojunction. The reasons attributed are – effective heterojunction formation between B@CN and S@CN; Incorporation of RGO which has capability to can enhance visible light absorption and migration of carriers; CQDs and plays an important role in the migration of photo-generated electrons. In addition coupling of CQDs with g-C3N4 can change the dynamics of optical and electrochemical response [50].

The Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 5c) for determining the flat band (FB) potentials were obtained from electrochemical studies for CN, S@CN and B@CN under 750 Hz. The Mott-Schottky equation is represented as follows
(1)
1
C 2
=
2
V app
−
V fb
−
kt e
N D
ε
ε o
e
A 2
where, k, C, T, Vfb, ND, e, ε0 and ε represent Boltzmann constant, capacitance, absolute temperature, flat band potential, donor density, electronic charge, dielectric constant in vacuum and dielectric constant of material respectively. The flat band (FB) potential (VFB) is then obtained by plotting C−2 vs applied potential (vs NHE) and extrapolating to C−2 = 0.

As can be observed from positive slopes of the three carbon nitride samples, the materials are n-type semiconductors. The VFB for CN, B@CN and S@CN are −0.92, −1.00 and −1.10 eV respectively (vs NHE at pH = 7). As reported earlier top of VB band for major class of semiconductors lies between 0.10 and 0.30 eV and for determining CB positions it's taken as 0.20 eV [51]. Thus the calculated conduction bands (ECB) are −1.12 (CN), −1.20 (B@CN) and −1.32 eV (S@CN).

The valence bands positions are then calculated using relation:
(2)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g

The VB potentials for CN, B@CN and S@CN are estimated as 1.58, 1.41 and 1.20 eV. The band gap parameters have been listed in Table S1. It can be observed that with doping the CB edges of CN move to more negative side while the VB edges are now less positive as compared to the bare CN.

The charge carrier separation and flow of electron-holes are necessary for efficient photocatalytic process. The electron transfer capacity was tested and determined from Nyquist plots obtained from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and results are presented in Fig. 6(a). In fact the impedance gets reduced if there is a high and smooth high transfer of electrons in the photocatalyst. When a heterojunction is formed from two or more semiconductors then a high interfacial charge transfer leads to formation of intrinsic electric field which further facilitates the movement on light exposure. This leads to fall in the recombination rate. It can be observed that as compared to bare g-C3N4 the radius of Nyquist plots for doped CN is lower showing that dopants reduce the recombination and facilitate charge flow. The arc radius in fact represents the electron-transfer resistance. S or B doping is conducive to decrease the resistance of the catalytic interface and improves or enhances the electron transfer. The radius is further decreased for binary junction SB-40 and for CRSB the radius is lowest which shows the electron transfer capacity is highest. The increase/decrease or shifts in the electrochemical impedance show that there are changes in the junction interfacial properties. With the effective formation of the junction between S@CN and B@CN the electrons and holes flow between the suitably placed conduction and valence bands. This increases the separation and reduces recombination. RGO and CQDs also contribute in the electron transfer as they can act as electron sink, transfer agents and source of electrons under visible light. The photo-current response was also analyzed for S@CN, B@CN and CRSB and results are given in Fig. S3. The higher photocurrent density often results in better photocatalytic performance [52,53]. The current time plots (for 5 cycles) show that photo-current response of CRSB is the highest as compared to the individual semiconductor counterparts. Pure SCN and BCN show a weak photo-current intensity due to low separation of electron-holes or high recombination. For CRSB the role of CQDs and RGO in charge separation and ease of flow for photogenerated electrons lead to high photocurrent. CQDs help in Z-scheme transfer mechanism with accumulation of electrons in CB of S@CN. A minor decrease in photocurrent is observed gradually because of the slow detachment of sample from the electrode surface with time. The reduction in recombination was also further confirmed by photoluminescence spectroscopy. The PL intensity is highest for g-C3N4 and follows the order g-C3N4 > S@g-C3N4 > B@g-C3N4 > SB-40 > CRSB. The mid-levels or impurity states originated from S or B doping in CN serve as electron-hole separation centers and not as recombination centers [54]. The electrons move from CB of B@C3N4 to CQD and holes from VB of S@C3N4 and recombine to ensure charge separation and flow. Thus PL intensity falls for CRSB photocatalyst. The recombination is lowest in case of CRSB photocatalyst and this is supported by EIS measurements.

The S@CN/B@CN junction was fabricated for overcoming shortcomings of pristine g-C3N4 and to attempt a hetero-assembly out of S and B doped carbon nitride. In addition, from EIS and PL results it has been proven that the recombination is reduced in case of assembly. However the ratios of the two should be optimized for best response and photocatalytic action. Firstly the ratio of S@CN and B@CN was optimized with varying the percentage of S@CN in B@CN and tested for photocatalytic degradation of CMP under visible light. The obtained results are presented in Fig. S4. The performance is as follows:
SB − 10
56.1 %
> SB − 20
58.2 %
> SB − 30
73.2 %
> SB − 40
80.4 %
> SB − 50
66.5 %

The doped CN shows a higher photocatalytic degradation of CMP which is due to higher visible absorption and reduced recombination. With junction formation between two types of doped g-C3N4 form photocatalytic activity is improved. When 10% S@CN is added to B@CN the CMP degradation increases from 50.2% to 58.3% (SB-10). This is due to better photocatalytic potential of sulphur doped CN. The gradual increase is observed till SB-40 after which the rate falls for SB-50. This is because at high dopant concentrations the dopants may soon start behaving as recombination centers and a fall in the free charge carriers is observed. Thus SB-40 was used further to optimize content of RGO (3–11%) for CMP degradation under the similar conditions (Fig. S5). The order of performance is as under
RSB
8 % RGO
80.1 %
> RSB
11 % RGO
77.6 %
> RSB
5 % RGO
70.8 %
> RSB
3 % RGO
66.5 %

On addition of RGO to SB-40 the degradation starts increasing and reaches up to 80.1% in 90 min for 8% RGO loading. With inclusion of RGO, adsorption is favored because of high surface area and functionality. However at excessive RGO loading the rate suddenly falls because it may hinder optical absorption of junction. The RSB with 8% loading was further utilized for CQD loading (0.5%) and termed as CRSB photocatalyst.

Before light assisted degradation, the tests were performed under dark and results are given in Fig. S6. In the dark no excitation is feasible so the removal of CMP is attributed to the adsorption capabilities of the photocatalysts. The CMP removal capacity in dark in a 1 h experiment follows following order:
CRSB
62.5 %
> SB − 40
44.2 %
> S @ CN
34.2 %
> B @ CN
31.3 %
> CN
28.6 %

The doped and bare g-C3N4 shows low but similar CMP removal capacity of ~30%, which increases to 62.5% in case of CRSB photocatalyst. The reason being that in case of CRSB more functionality is present in form of CN and RGO. In addition, as seen earlier CRSB has a higher surface area, which facilitates adsorption. Adsorption plays an important role in pollutant removal [55,56]. Keeping in mind the adsorption capacity, further tests were studied in two protocols: 1 h dark adsorption followed by light exposure and direct exposure. When the two test sets were compared (Fig. S7 and 7a), it was found that under first test set (1 h dark experiment followed by visible light exposure nearly 90% removal was obtained for CRSB in 120 min, while direct exposure leads to 99.2% CMP removal in 90 min. This indicates that direct exposure gives better results. This is due to the fact that under first condition the pollutant molecules first get adsorbed onto photocatalyst in dark and cover the surface of the catalyst. This may inhibit the light exposure to the catalyst and thus decrease the activity. However under direct exposure the CMP molecules get adsorbed and get simultaneously degraded by radicals generated on surface. Further experiments were done for direct exposure protocol. For doped CN the degradation is higher because interstitial doping of heteroatoms as B and S into pristine g-C3N4 can provide new channels to facilitate or mediate the migration of electrons-holes via bridging which increases the separation and increase the photocatalytic activity. Also, the electronic structure of CN gets modified on doping to capture more visible light. Figure 7(a) shows that highest degradation is achieved for CRSB photocatalyst. The performance is as follows:
CRSB
99.1 %

80.4 %

As compared to bare CN there is a huge change in the degradation potential obtained in case of CRSB. The photo-degradation rate fits into pseudo-first order kinetics as shown in Figure 7(b). The CMP degradation rate for CRSB photocatalyst is ~10 times faster than bare g-C3N4 without any use of metallic catalyst or sensitizer. The rate constants are as follows:

High performance of CRSB catalyst can be attributed to following reasons: Effective junction formation between S@CN and B@CN; Better adsorption potential of RGO in addition to g-C3N4, higher surface area, enhance visible activity; decreased recombination and high charge flow via RGO and CQDs. The detailed mechanisms are discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction parameters and solar performance
ce:section.ce:para: The role of pH is highly important for interactions between the pollutant and the catalyst [57,58]. Firstly, the effect of pH onto CMP degradation by CRSB photocatalyst under visible light was studied as chemistry of drugs is extremely sensitive to pH. The photocatalytic experiments also dependent on the surface charge of the photocatalysts which were determined by measuring zeta potentials. At neutral pH the zeta potentials for S@CN, B@CN, RGO and CRSB are found to be −15.33 mV, −15.77 mV, +0.22 mV and -1.22 mV. At working pH = 6 the zeta potentials for S@CN, B@CN, RGO and CRSB are decreased to −4.34 mV, −4.65 mV, + 4.33 mV and +2.56 mV. The results are presented in Fig. 7(c). The pKa value for CMP is 5.5 [59] and pzc for CRSB (Fig. S8) is 6.5. At pH = 6, the CMP exists mostly in anionic form and surface of CRSB catalyst is +vely charged (as observed from zeta potentials). This makes better ionic or electrostatic interactions between the two and hence higher adsorption. The degradation is highest at pH = 6–7. At extremely acidic and basic conditions both the pollutant and catalyst are similar charged and electrostatic repulsions lead to poor activity. In addition high acidity may also cause leaching of the catalyst. Also at extremely basic condition, the hydroxyl ions compete with the drug molecule for adsorption.

Water also contains many inorganic ions which may affect the photocatalytic reactions by interfering with the active oxygen species generation or the antibiotic behavior. The effect of common ions as Cl−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, Ca2+ and Na+ (all with concentration of 0.05 M) were studied on CMP degradation in presence of CRSB under visible light as shown in Fig. 7(d). Chloride and Na+ ions have no adverse effect on the degradation rate with a little rate fall for Cl− because of formation of HOCl●- which is less reactive as compared to ●OH. A decrease in degradation rate is observed for HCO3 – ions because they tend to form less reactive ●CO3 −. However degradation is expedited for NO3 − ions as they can generate ●OH radicals under visible exposure [60]. Scheme 1 shows the reactions involving the common inorganic ions during photocatalysis. Ca2+ also decreases the CMP degradation rate because they tend to form complexes with the drug molecule.

Further confirmation was made by varying the water matrix for CMP degradation (Fig. 7e). Table S2 shows the parameters of municipal tap water used. CMP was hardly removed without catalyst in tap and distilled water. However, the degradation falls to a little extent in presence of municipal tap water (CRSB + TW k
app = 0.06221 min −1 ) because of obvious presence of various electrolytes and natural organic matter which also hinders the optical absorption. The degradation was also tested under sunlight to assess the practical and large scale capabilities of the photocatalyst (Fig. 7f and 8a). The order of performance in a 120 min experiment is: CRSB (92.4%) > SB-40 (74.3%) > S@g-C
3
N
4
(48.3%) > B@g-C3N4 (45.6%) > g-C3N4 (41.1%). Though a high degradation is achieved but the time of exposure required is stretched. This obvious decrease in the photocatalytic rate is observed owing to due to lower energy of natural solar light. The rate constant for CRSB + solar system is 0.05122 min-1.. The high degradation of CMP under natural sunlight shows that ternary catalyst CRSB is capable of working under large scale practical conditions too. Fig. 8(c) shows the comparison of rate constants under different conditions for CMP degradation with CRSB as catalyst. Under visible light kapp under direct exposure + visible + CRSB system is highest under normal conditions. However in presence of electrolytes kapp (CRSB + visible + NO3 − system) is highest showing highest degradation rate.

For confirming the mineralization of CMP, LC-MS analysis was performed after 15 and 45 min of photodegradation under CRSB + visible system. The reconstituted LC-mass spectra are provided in Fig. S9 and the structure of some major intermediates detected is listed in Table S3. As per the intermediates detected a degradation mechanism has been proposed as per Scheme 2 . The basic and most common route is divided into three pathways. First is the degradation via CN bond cleavage to form product (m/z = 195) [61] with loss of dichloroacetamide and further dehydration forms end product (m/z = 179). Also CN bond cleavage also generates 4-(2-amino-1, 3-dihydroxy-propanyl)-nitrobenzene by-product with m/z = 196 by loosing dichloroacetic acid [62].

Secondly dechloridation by radical attack leads to product with m/z = 224 which via further subsequent oxidations forms 4-nitrobenzoic acid (m/z = 167), 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (m/z = 123) and nitrobenzene (m/z = 123). The oxidation of product (m/z = 224) also leads to removal of nitro group and form 4-hydroxy benzoic acid (m/z = 138). Further via ring opening products as m/z = 212 are formed and dichloroacetic acid (m/z = 129) is produced which forms simpler molecules as oxalic acid for aldehydes. Similar intermediates and products were also reported by several works [63,64]. By subsequent attack by radicals the drug can ultimately mineralize into low weight molecules as HCl, C2H5OH, Cl2, water and CO2 etc.

The photocatalytic degradation of CMP is obviously based on role of electrons, holes and reactive oxygen species generated on irradiation i.e. hydroxyl radical (●OH), superoxide radical anion (●O2 −). Hence for deeper insight of the degradation of CMP by prepared photocatalyst has been gained here using scavenging experiments. To scavenge the active species, isopropanol for ●OH radicals, EDTA for photogenerated h+ and benzoquinone (BQ) for ●O2 – were used during CMP degradation under visible + CRSB system. The results are presented in Fig. 8(c). It can be observed that degradation falls sharply in presence of BQ showing that ●O2 – plays a major role in degradation as the rate decrease is not high in case of IPA. The scavenging of electrons also leads to decrease in CMP degradation because they play an important role in production of superoxide radical anion. However, still hydroxyl radicals are active in degradation. These observations can be further ascertained by understanding the junction properties, possible charge flow, thermodynamic feasibility of radical formation and ESR measurements. The junction formed between S@CN and B@CN with the possible electron-hole movements and role of CQDs & RGO is represented in Fig. 9 . Under visible irradiation both S@CN and B@CN absorb light leading to double transfer of e− and h+ between CB and VB. In absence of CQD as a bridging link, the binary junction behaves as a typical type-II junction with electrons moving from CB of S@CN (−1.32 eV) to that of B@CN (−1.20 eV) which is less negative. Similarly the photogenerated holes can migrate from more +ve VB of B@CN (1.40 eV) to VB of S@CN (1.20 eV). However inclusion of CQDs changes the scenario. They act as a bridge for transmission of charge as can be seen in Fig. 9. The excited electrons from CB of B@C3N4 and holes from VB of S@C3N4 get transferred to CQDs. This leads to accumulation of electrons on CB of S@C3N4 and VB of B@C3N4 for production of •O2 – and ●OH radicals respectively. The electrons are trapped by the CQDS from CB of B@CN and recombine with holes from S@CN via a Z-scheme pathway. (B@C3N4 → CQDs → S@C3N4).This is in accordance with previously reported works with a similar observation [65,66]. Thus, there are a separation of charges and a successful junction formation out of two differently abled carbon nitrides.

For confirming the possibilities of reactive oxygen species generation, the redox potentials are to be considered in reference to the band potentials. As far as •O2 – radical generation is considered, the CB edges of both S@CN and B@CN are highly negative as compared to redox potential E(O2/•O2 −) = −0.33 eV [11]. These CB potential of S@C3N4 where the electrons are concentrated is more negative than bare CN which means that thermodynamically the feasibility of •O2 – production is higher in doped CN. Also for accumulated electrons on CB of S@CN the potential is quite high for high production of superoxide radical anions. Considering the redox potential E (•OH/H2O) is 2.27 eV vs NHE and E (•OH/−OH) is 1.99 eV vs NHE, the feasibility of ●OH radicals generation is less because of lesser positive VB potential of both S@CN and B@CN. However as per scavenging experiments a contribution from hydroxyl radicals can be observed. This means that ●OH radicals are formed by some alternative route as per Scheme 3 :

In addition the redox potential for H2O2 production is E (O2/H2O2) = −0.695 eV, so feasibility of H2O2 is higher here because of highly negative CB of S@C3N4. The presence of the radicals was also confirmed by ESR measurements for DMPO trapped •O2 – and •OH radicals and results are given in Fig. 9(d). These studies have been done for CMP degradation under CRSB + visible system. It is clearly visible that intensity of DMPO-•O2 – signal is high as compared to DMPO-•OH. This means that the superoxide radical anions are present in higher amount and are main degradation responsible species. These observations are supported by scavenging experiments and junction analysis. The high performance of CRSB catalyst as compared to SB and carbon nitrides needs to be analyzed and role of each moiety forming the composite catalyst has to be ascertained. Apart from charge separation, dopants, RGO and CQDs play an important role in exceptional degradation performance for an all metal free photocatalyst. The P and B as dopants can also act as recombination centers for free electrons. This also helps in separation of charges for further reactions. As observed earlier the visible absorption potential of doped CN is also higher. Moreover, because of modification by RGO and CQDs visible absorption is enhanced for the junction leading to increased excitation of electrons. The Fermi level of RGO is located at −0.08 eV [67]. It can behave as an electron sink in addition to mediator/facilitator and adsorbent. The photogenerated electrons, in addition to migration to CB levels, may accumulate on surface of RGO where subsequent exposure generates more reactive oxygen species. The extra electrons which may lead to recombination may thus be utilized and thus higher potential conduction bands are protected for photocatalysis. Also RGO provides mechanical support which is good for better dispersion of CN, functionality and large surface area for better pollutant adsorption and hence faster degradation. Accordingly the Fermi levels of both RGO and CQDs lie at −0.08 eV which are more positive than that of carbon nitrides. Thus when an intimate contact is formed between RGO, CN and CQDs, a Schottky barrier is formed for electrons to pass which causes shift in Fermi level. Thus the Fermi level of CN gets shifted to a + ve potential till equilibrium, which shortens the migration path [50].

In addition the photogenerated electrons form B@CN and holes from S@CN also migrate to CQDs for recombination, keeping the high potential CB of S@CN (−1.32 eV) free for attacking oxygen to form •O2 – radicals. This essentially transforms the ordinary double charge transfer mechanism to an efficient Z-scheme. The accumulated electrons tend to escape to RGO and CQDs also for simultaneous oxygen reduction. Additionally, the up-conversion effect from CQDs also increases the light absorption capacity of photocatalyst. On analyzing the role of each component of CRSB junction and structural aspects, it can be explained as follows:
•
Dopants: B and S doped carbon nitrides form an efficient junction with different band structures for charge transfer, dopants contribute in reducing the recombination
•
The nanosheets of CN have short diffusion length, high surface area and visible performance
•
Due to high mobility and conductivity of electrons along RGO sheets, it behaves as e− sink/mediator - reduced recombination and easy diffusion of e−
•
High surface area and functionality of RGO facilitates adsorption of CMP and better degradation
•
CQDs with up-conversion effect, high visible absorption, wide spectral response and charge separation
•
High potential CB of both the doped carbon nitrides capable of high production of •O2 – radicals
•
Z-scheme transfer- CQDs and RGO aids in high potential conduction band of S@CN for electrons to be free for O2 attack

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability, TOC analysis and cyto-toxicity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: For practical and long term use photocatalyst must be stable and reusable. Fig. 10(a) shows the The reusability of CRSB catalyst for CMP degradation in visible light for 5 repeated. It can be observed that there is no large decrease in activity showing that CRSB is stable, sustainable and reusable. To further confirm this XRD and SEM analysis of CRSB catalyst was made and results are given in Fig. 10(b) and (c) respectively. There are no abnormal changes in XRD pattern of CRSB with all the characteristic peaks observed. The SEM image of CRSB after degradation experiments shows no changes in the morphology, structure and shape. The mineralization was further confirmed by total organic carbon (TOC) analysis. Fig. S10 shows TOC removal for CMP degradation with CRSB for 4 h in visible and solar light under direct exposure. 88% and 70% TOC removal was achieved in just 4 h exposure under visible and solar light respectively. The toxicity of degradation products and extent of mineralization was also tested by cyto-toxicity study of photocatalytic treated CMP contaminated water on PBL cells by MTT assay method. MTT assay is a tetrazolium salt (pale yellow) and is reduced into formazan (dark blue) by mitochondrial dehydrogenases. Fig. 10 (d) shows the bar shows the %cell viability in untreated cells, ConA (Concanavalin A) as positive control and photocatalytically treated water obtained after CMP degradation. The cell viability is obviously high for Cona (95.1%), and 85.2% for untreated cells. For cells treated with CMP mineralized products, the cell viability is still 84.4%. This confirms the mineralization of CMP into non-toxic products. The treated water did not harm the functions of mitochondria and viability of human blood cells. This can be clearly observed in digital images of treated PBL cells Fig. 10 (e), (f) and (g), where no increased debris of dead cells is observed for degraded products treated cells. Thus CRSB catalyst is sustainable metal free stable photocatalyst with high performance and high mineralization potential.

In conclusion, highly visible active metal free self-assembled photo-catalytic hetero-assembly CRSB was prepared by using B@C3N4/S@C3N4 junction with different band structure. On the basis of optical response, electrochemical impedance and photo-luminescence results the binary assembly of two differently doped carbon nitrides has been successfully formed via rational optimization. With introduction of RGO and CQDs in optimized amounts, CRSB catalyst (with 8% RGO) shows a high charge separation, electron flow, ●O2 – radical generation and rapid removal of CMP as pollutant. CRSB shows almost complete removal (99.1%) of CMP under visible light in 1 h and 92.4% under natural solar light in 2 h. This excellent performance of an all metal free photocatalyst is attributed to an effective charge separation via transfer of electrons and holes among bands, RGO and CQDs. RGO sheets due to high surface area, adsorption potential facilitates high adsorption of drug onto catalyst and leads to better charge diffusion and separation. CQD have up-conversion effect, increases the spectral response and effectively converts a double charged mechanism into a Z-scheme transfer. Superoxide radical anions are identified as active species using radical scavengers, band structure analysis and ESR measurements. High mineralization was ascertained by faster TOC reduction and negligible cyto-toxicity on human blood cells. With unique structural benefits of this all metal free catalyst utilizing a novel junction with high light harvesting for visible and solar driven degradation of CMP antibiotic. This work promises the required shift to bio-benevolent photocatalysts for environmental decontamination.
